Abstract: Translation averaging aims to recover camera locations from pairwise relative translation directions and is a fundamental component of global Structure-from-Motion pipelines. The problem is challenging because direction measurements contain no distance information, making the estimation problem highly ill-conditioned and highly sensitive to corrupted observations. In this paper, we propose TriP, a triangle-based framework for robust translation averaging. TriP first infers local relative edge scales from triangle geometry, and then synchronizes the scales of overlapping triangles in the logarithmic domain to recover globally consistent edge lengths and camera locations. By leveraging higher-order consistency across triangles, the proposed method is robust to adversarial, cycle-consistent, and other structured corruptions. In addition, TriP avoids the collapse issue without requiring any extra anti-collapse constraints, since log-scale synchronization excludes the degenerate zero-scale solution by construction. These structural advantages enable a particularly strong theory for exact location recovery. On the practical side, TriP is fully parallelizable, computationally efficient, and naturally scalable to graphs with millions of cameras. Moreover, it outperforms all previous translation averaging methods by a large margin on both synthetic and real datasets.

</details>


### [30] [UniV2D: Bridging Visual Restoration and Semantic Perception for Underwater Salient Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07146)
*Laibin Chang,Shaodong Wang,Yunke Wang,Xu Zhang,Kui Jiang,Chang Xu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniV2D improves underwater salient object detection by jointly optimizing visual restoration and detection, overcoming limitations of traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for effective underwater salient object detection amidst challenges like visual degradation motivates a new approach that resolves semantic inconsistency in traditional methods.

Method: UniV2D is a Unified Vision-to-Detection Network with a hierarchical dual-branch architecture that incorporates a self-calibrated decoder, a mask-aware restoration module, and a saliency-guided refinement module.

Result: UniV2D shows significant improvements in performance on various benchmarks, surpassing existing state-of-the-art techniques both quantitatively and qualitatively.

Conclusion: UniV2D establishes a new standard for joint underwater perception by effectively integrating visual restoration and salient object detection.

Abstract: Underwater salient object detection (USOD) plays a vital role in marine vision tasks but remains fundamentally challenging due to severe visual degradation, such as selective absorption and medium scattering. Conventional pipelines typically adopt a sequential "enhance-then-detect" paradigm. However, isolating low-level visual restoration from high-level semantic perception often leads to semantic inconsistency, where the restored images may not be optimal for detection and can even introduce task-irrelevant noise. To break this sequential bottleneck, we propose UniV2D, a Unified Vision-to-Detection Network that jointly optimizes visual restoration and salient object detection within a mutually beneficial framework. Unlike traditional methods that rely on disjointed pipelines or rigid physical priors, UniV2D introduces a semantic-driven learning paradigm: high-level saliency semantics actively guide the restoration process, while the restored visual cues reciprocally enhance saliency perception. Specifically, UniV2D features a hierarchical dual-branch architecture. It first employs a self-calibrated decoder to predict initial saliency masks alongside a mask-aware restoration module to reconstruct image content. Subsequently, a saliency-guided refinement module equipped with cross-level modulation is utilized to align structural fidelity with semantic consistency. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that UniV2D significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations, establishing a new standard for joint underwater perception.

</details>


### [31] [Uncovering and Shaping the Latent Representation of 3D Scene Topology in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07148)
*Haoming Wang,Wei Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper investigates the spatial reasoning abilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and shows they have a latent 3D topological map, which can be enhanced using a new regularization method, leading to better performance on spatial tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore whether Vision-Language Models create a 3D internal representation akin to human cognitive maps, given their success with 2D egocentric inputs.

Method: The authors extract a spatial subspace from VLMs through linear feature extraction, relate it to the Laplacian eigenmaps of 3D Gaussian-kernel graphs, and apply a Dirichlet energy-based regularization during fine-tuning.

Result: Experiments confirm the existence of a latent topological map in VLMs, and the proposed regularization enhances performance significantly on spatial understanding tasks, achieving up to 12.1% better results compared to standard methods.

Conclusion: The study establishes a connection between VLMs and geometric representations of 3D space, offering a new regularization technique that improves their performance in scene topology comprehension.

Abstract: Decades of cognitive science establish that humans navigate environments by forming cognitive maps, defined as allocentric and topology-preserving representations of 3D space. While modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate emergent spatial reasoning from 2D egocentric inputs, it remains unclear whether they construct an analogous 3D internal representation. In this paper, we demonstrate that current VLMs do possess a latent topological map of 3D scenes, but it is heavily overshadowed by non-geometric visual semantics, such as color and shape. By isolating this spatial subspace through cross-scene linear feature extraction, we extract a clean spatial subspace that causally controls the model's spatial outputs. We mathematically shape this latent representation and prove its correspondence to the Laplacian eigenmaps of the scene's 3D Gaussian-kernel graph, converging to the physical 3D space in the continuous limit. Motivated by this geometric identification, we further introduce a mathematically principled latent regularization method for VLMs, based on Dirichlet energy. Applying this single-term regularizer to a minimal 500-step supervised VLM fine-tuning (SFT) on simple synthetic data yields significant improvements on real-world spatial benchmarks, outperforming standard SFT and competitive baselines by up to 12.1\% in spatial tasks involving scene topology understanding. Source code is available at https://github.com/pittisl/vlm-latent-shaping

</details>


### [32] [Real-IAD MVN: A Multi-View Normal Vector Dataset and Benchmark for High-Fidelity Industrial Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07149)
*Wenbing Zhu,Jianing Liang,Linjie Cheng,Yurui Pan,Zhuhao Chen,Qingwang Yan,Yudong Cheng,Jianghui Zhang,Mingmin Chi,Bo Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Real-IAD-MVN, a new large-scale industrial dataset designed to enhance the detection of subtle geometric anomalies in industrial settings by providing high-fidelity surface normal maps from multiple viewpoints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for Industrial Anomaly Detection struggle to identify subtle geometric defects due to limitations in standard 2D imaging and sparse 3D data.

Method: The authors developed Real-IAD-MVN, which captures detailed surface normal maps from five viewpoints, and introduced a baseline reconstruction method to validate the dataset and establish benchmarks.

Result: Experiments show that using dense, multi-view surface normals significantly improves detection performance over sparse 3D point clouds, and that the unified prototype method outperforms current multimodal fusion techniques.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that Real-IAD-MVN is a valuable resource for improving geometric anomaly detection, demonstrating significant advancements in detection capabilities.

Abstract: Industrial Anomaly Detection (IAD) is critical for quality control, but existing methods struggle with subtle, geometric defects. Standard 2D (RGB) images are sensitive to texture and lighting but often miss fine geometric anomalies. While 3D point clouds capture macro-shape, they are typically too sparse to detect micro-defects like scratches or pits. We address this fundamental data limitation by introducing Real-IAD-MVN (Multi-View Normal), a large-scale industrial dataset. By upgrading our acquisition system, Real-IAD-MVN captures high-fidelity surface normal maps from five distinct viewpoints, replacing sparse 3D data entirely. This provides a comprehensive geometric representation at a micro-detail level, making previously invisible side-wall and occluded defects explicitly detectable. Our experiments, conducted on this new dataset, first provide evidence that incorporating dense, multi-view pseudo-3D (surface normals) yields significantly better detection performance than using sparse 3D point cloud data. To further validate the dataset and provide a strong benchmark, we introduce a baseline method based on reconstruction, which learns to extract cross-modal unified prototypes from the image and normal map streams. We demonstrate that this unified prototype approach surpasses existing state-of-the-art multimodal fusion methods, highlighting the rich potential of our new dataset for advancing geometric anomaly detection.

</details>


### [33] [PRIMED: Adaptive Modality Suppression for Referring Audio-Visual Segmentation via Biased Competition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07154)
*Yuchen He,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PRIMED addresses the challenges of Referring Audio-Visual Segmentation by adaptively modeling modality relevance and improving accuracy through innovative techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to enhance the performance of Ref-AVS by overcoming the limitations of existing methods that inadequately process varying importance across modalities.

Method: PRIMED utilizes a Modality Prior Decoder to assess modality reliance and guide attention, along with a Token Distiller for global visual token extraction and a Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss for improved discrimination.

Result: Experimental results on the Ref-AVS benchmark show that PRIMED achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The proposed approach successfully improves Ref-AVS performance by fostering adaptive modality suppression and enhancing context-aware fusion.

Abstract: Referring Audio-Visual Segmentation (Ref-AVS) seeks to localize and segment target objects in video frames based on visual, auditory, and textual referring cues. The task is challenging because the relevance of different modalities varies across referring expressions and scenes, while existing methods typically treat multimodal cues as homogeneous inputs for fusion, prompting, or reasoning, making them vulnerable to irrelevant or misleading modalities. To address this problem, we propose PRIMED, inspired by the biased competition theory in cognitive neuroscience, which explicitly models both visual perception and language-driven prior modulation, and enables more accurate Ref-AVS by adaptive modality suppression. Specifically, a Modality Prior Decoder first estimates whether the referring expression relies primarily on audio, vision, or their joint interaction, generating a modality prior to adaptively guide high-level attention. A Token Distiller further extracts compact global visual tokens from high-level features and shares them across Competition-aware Cross-modal Fusion modules to provide hierarchical global context. Additionally, we introduce a Spatial-Aware Semantic Alignment loss to further enhance foreground-background discrimination through contrastive learning. Extensive experiments on the Ref-AVS benchmark demonstrate that PRIMED achieves state-of-the-art overall performance.

</details>


### [34] [Hierarchical Perfusion Graphs for Tumor Heterogeneity Modeling in Glioma Molecular Subtyping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07156)
*Han Jang,Junhyeok Lee,Heeseong Eum,Joon Jang,Yoseob Han,Seung Hong Choi,Kyu Sung Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HiPerfGNN framework enhances non-invasive glioma molecular subtyping using MRI by integrating hemodynamic signatures with radiogenomic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve non-invasive molecular subtyping of gliomas, reducing reliance on invasive tissue sampling by utilizing advanced imaging techniques.

Method: The HiPerfGNN framework employs a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to derive hemodynamic representations from DSC MRI, which are then analyzed using a hierarchical graph neural network for molecular subtype prediction.

Result: The model achieved high AUC scores (0.96 for IDH, 0.89 for 1p/19q, and 0.84 for WHO grade) in an internal cohort and maintained robust performance on an independent external cohort.

Conclusion: Integrating perfusion dynamics into radiogenomics enhances glioma molecular subtype prediction and offers a promising non-invasive diagnostic approach.

Abstract: Precise molecular subtyping of gliomas, including isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) mutation and 1p/19q codeletion, directly guides surgical and therapeutic decisions, yet currently relies on invasive tissue sampling. Deep learning on structural MRI has emerged as a non-invasive alternative, but anatomy-only approaches cannot capture the hemodynamic signatures that distinguish molecular subtypes. Radiogenomics based on dynamic susceptibility contrast (DSC) MRI holds immense potential for non-invasively characterizing glioma molecular subtypes, yet clinical deployment has been hindered by inter-site variability and the limitations of voxel-wise analysis. We introduce HiPerfGNN, a framework that first learns discrete hemodynamic representations from raw time-intensity curves using a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE). These quantized perfusion codes define coarse-level graph nodes representing functional tumor habitats, each of which is hierarchically subdivided into fine-level subregions guided by structural MRI. A hierarchical graph neural network then propagates information across scales for molecular prediction. On an internal cohort (n=475), the model achieved AUCs of 0.96 (IDH), 0.89 (1p/19q), and 0.84 (WHO grade), and maintained robust IDH performance (AUC 0.89) on an independent external cohort (n=397) without recalibration. Gradient-based saliency analysis confirms biologically grounded attention patterns aligned with known glioma pathophysiology. Our results demonstrate the added value of integrating perfusion dynamics into radiogenomic pipelines for glioma molecular subtyping. Code is available at https://github.com/janghana/HiPerfGNN.

</details>


### [35] [Masks Can Talk: Extracting Structured Text Information from Single-Modal Images for Remote Sensing Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07178)
*Kai Zheng,Hang-Cheng Dong,Jiatong Pan,Zhenkai Wu,Fupeng Wei,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces S2M, a framework for multimodal change detection in remote sensing that utilizes structured textual features derived from existing change labels, enhancing accuracy in identifying land-cover changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings of unimodal and existing multimodal approaches in change detection, which fail to effectively leverage the information encoded in ground-truth masks.

Method: S2M generates structured textual features from change masks, transcribing regions into semantic quadruples and creating fixed-template descriptions. It employs a two-stage training strategy, fine-tuning on remote sensing images followed by a multimodal decoder with a bi-directional contrastive loss.

Result: S2M outperforms existing methods on the newly created Gaza-Change-v2 dataset, achieving a Sek of 17.80% and an F$_{scd}$ of 66.14%, surpassing multimodal methods that use large language models.

Conclusion: The study illustrates that ground-truth masks can effectively serve as a source of structured information for improving change detection methodologies.

Abstract: Remote sensing change detection is pivotal for urban monitoring, disaster assessment, and environmental resource management. Yet, unimodal deep learning methods frequently confuse genuine semantic changes with visually similar but irrelevant variations. Recent multimodal approaches incorporate text as auxiliary supervision, but their descriptions are either semantically coarse and unstructured or model-generated and thus noisy. Critically, all of them overlook a simple fact: fine-grained change semantics are already implicitly encoded in the ground-truth mask labels that come standard with every change detection dataset. These masks know where the change happened, what the land-cover types were before and after, how the transition occurred, and how many objects were involved. In this paper, we propose S2M, a framework that obtains structured textual features directly from change labels at zero additional annotation cost. Specifically, each change region is automatically transcribed into a semantic quadruple (where, what, how, how many) and converted into several fixed-template text descriptions, providing precise, dense, and noise-free multimodal supervision. We adopts a two-stage training strategy to fine-tune on remote sensing imagery firstly for robust domain-specific representation, after which a multimodal decoder with a bi-directional contrastive loss is introduced to achieve deep alignment between visual features and structured textual embeddings. To validate our method, we construct Gaza-Change-v2, a new multi-class change detection (MCD) dataset about the Gaza Strip. On this MCD dataset, S2M achieves a Sek of 17.80\% and an F$_{\text{scd}}$ of 66.14\%, notably surpassing even multimodal methods that leverage large language models. Our work demonstrates that masks can indeed talk. They tell us exactly what, where, how, and how many changes have occurred.

</details>


### [36] [SatSurfGS: Generalizable 2D Gaussian Splatting for Sparse-View Satellite Surface Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07181)
*Min Chen,Wei Guo,Bin Wang,Wen Li,Tong Fang,Jinbo Zhang,Junqi Zhao,Hong Kuang,Han Hu,Xuming Ge,Qing Zhu,Bo Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes SatSurfGS, a novel method for sparse-view satellite image reconstruction using 2D Gaussian Splatting, addressing challenges associated with multi-view matching reliability and photometric differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse-view satellite image surface reconstruction is hindered by spatially heterogeneous multi-view matching reliability due to various visual complexities.

Method: SatSurfGS employs a coarse-to-fine Gaussian attribute prediction framework, incorporating modules for confidence-aware feature fusion, self-consistency refinement, and differentiated supervision allocation.

Result: The method demonstrates enhanced rendering quality, improved surface reconstruction accuracy, better cross-dataset generalization, and increased inference efficiency on satellite datasets compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed SatSurfGS method advances the state-of-the-art in satellite image reconstruction by effectively modeling geometric reliability and leveraging multi-view information.

Abstract: Sparse-view satellite image surface reconstruction remains highly challenging, fundamentally because the reliability of multi-view matching under satellite imaging conditions is strongly spatially heterogeneous. Affected by large photometric differences, weak textures, and repetitive textures, multi-view geometric constraints are often sparse, unevenly distributed, and locally unreliable. Although 2D Gaussian Splatting (2DGS) is more suitable than 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for the explicit representation of continuous surfaces, research on generalizable feed-forward 2DGS frameworks for sparse-view satellite surface reconstruction is still lacking. To address this issue, we propose SatSurfGS, a generalizable sparse-view surface reconstruction method for satellite imagery based on 2DGS. The proposed method builds a coarse-to-fine Gaussian attribute prediction framework and explicitly models local geometric reliability at three levels: feature learning, Gaussian parameter estimation, and training optimization. Specifically, we propose a confidence-aware monocular multi-view feature fusion module to adaptively integrate monocular priors and multi-view matching features according to local confidence; a cross-stage self-consistency residual guidance module to stabilize stage-wise Gaussian parameter refinement using the residual between the rendered height map from the previous stage and the current-stage MVS height map, together with confidence information; and a confidence bidirectional routing loss to achieve differentiated allocation of geometric and appearance supervision. Experiments on satellite datasets show that the proposed method achieves improved rendering quality, surface reconstruction accuracy, cross-dataset generalization, and inference efficiency compared with representative generalizable baselines and competitive per-scene optimization methods.

</details>


### [37] [PicoEyes: Unified Gaze Estimation Framework for Mixed Reality with a Large-Scale Multi-View Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07188)
*Fuxin Duan,Hui Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PicoEyes is a gaze estimation framework that predicts multiple gaze attributes from monocular or binocular inputs and supports 3D eye reconstruction. It includes a comprehensive dataset and outperforms existing methods across various settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a unified system that accurately estimates gaze parameters and supports multiple input types and conditions in mixed reality applications.

Method: PicoEyes utilizes an end-to-end approach to predict gaze attributes, facilitating calibration and forecasting, while introducing a multi-view near-eye dataset with diverse annotations.

Result: PicoEyes achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing traditional gaze tracking methods in various scenarios such as no calibration and rewear after calibration.

Conclusion: This framework provides a robust, practical solution for gaze estimation, enhancing performance in mixed reality environments.

Abstract: We present PicoEyes, a unified gaze estimation framework that directly predicts all key attributes of gaze, including 3D eye parameters, eye-region segmentation, optical axis, visual axis, and depth maps, from either monocular or binocular inputs. The framework simultaneously addresses calibration, gaze forecasting, and varying device postures, while also supporting 3D eye reconstruction via joint estimation of eye parameters and depth maps in an end-to-end manner. In addition, we introduce a large-scale multi-view near-eye dataset containing comprehensive 2D and 3D annotations under diverse conditions, including train, test, rewear-test, and calibration sessions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PicoEyes achieves state-ofthe-art performance, consistently outperforming both academic and industrial gaze tracking methods across nocalibration, calibration, rewear-after-calibration, and forecasting settings. This work establishes a practical, end-toend paradigm for robust and generalizable gaze estimation in mixed reality (MR) applications.

</details>


### [38] [Attention Transfer Is Not Universally Effective for Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07191)
*Huaiyuan Qin,Muli Yang,Gabriel James Goenawan,Peng Hu,Chen Gong,Xi Peng,Hongyuan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Attention Transfer from teacher ViTs to student ViTs shows varied effectiveness across different model families, with architectural mismatch as a crucial factor for success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the effectiveness of Attention Transfer across different Vision Transformer (ViT) families, and understand why some fail despite successful transfers in others.

Method: The study analyzes 20 teacher models from 11 ViT families, assessing transfer success rates and conducting controlled analyses to pinpoint failure mechanisms.

Result: 7 out of 11 ViT families successfully transfer attention patterns, while 4 families demonstrate a consistent failure, leading to performance drops compared to no-transfer baselines. Architectural mismatch is identified as a key issue, resolved by adapting student architectures to include teacher-specific components.

Conclusion: Attention Transfer efficacy is contingent on matching the student architecture to that of the teacher; merely aligning attention patterns is insufficient if architectural compatibilities are not addressed.

Abstract: A recent work shows that Attention Transfer, which transfers only the attention patterns from a pre-trained teacher Vision Transformer (ViT) to a randomly initialized standard student ViT, is sufficient to recover the full benefit of the teacher's pre-trained weights. We revisit this finding on a comprehensive benchmark of 20 teachers from 11 well-known ViT families and reveal that Attention Transfer is not universally effective. While 7 families transfer successfully, 4 consistently fail, falling up to 5.1\% below the from-scratch no-transfer baseline. Further results demonstrate that this failure is family-consistent across model sizes, and persists under extended training durations, different transfer datasets, and out-of-distribution evaluations. Controlled analyses then consistently localize the problem to the attention-routing channel, indicating that the key issue is not whether the student can match the teacher's attention patterns, but whether the matched patterns remain functional for the student. Crucially, we identify architectural mismatch between the pre-trained teacher and the standard student as the primary mechanism. By adding only the teacher's native architectural components to the student in a randomly initialized state, we completely reverse the failure for all 4 families. Notably, these components alone do not improve from-scratch training, confirming that they specifically unlock the usability of the teacher's attention. We further systematically show that this failure is not explained by the inadequate choice of transfer loss or by differences in pre-training recipes. Our findings refine the prevailing understanding of attention in ViT representations: attention is sufficient \textit{only} when the student architecture matches the teacher.

</details>


### [39] [AsyncEvGS: Asynchronous Event-Assisted Gaussian Splatting for Handheld Motion-Blurred Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07192)
*Jun Dai,Renbiao Jin,Bo Xu,Yutian Chen,Linning Xu,Mulin Yu,Tianfan Xue,Shi Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel dual-camera system for 3D reconstruction that effectively handles motion blur using high-resolution RGB and event data, leading to improved photorealism in reconstructed images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current 3D reconstruction methods when dealing with motion blur in input images, particularly in practical handheld scenarios.

Method: Introduction of a high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event dual-camera system combined with a reconstruction framework that utilizes event data for sharp image reconstruction and employs a cross-domain pose estimation module for robust initialization.

Result: The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both a newly created RGB-Event dataset and established benchmarks, significantly enhancing reconstruction reliability under severe motion blur.

Conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates the feasibility and advantages of integrating high-resolution event data with traditional RGB imaging for improved 3D reconstruction.

Abstract: 3D reconstruction methods such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) achieve impressive photorealism but fail when input images suffer from severe motion blur. While event cameras provide high-temporal-resolution motion cues, existing event-assisted approaches rely on low-resolution sensors and strict synchronization, limiting their practicality for handheld 3D capture on common devices, such as smartphones. We introduce a flexible, high-resolution asynchronous RGB-Event dual-camera system and a corresponding reconstruction framework. Our approach first reconstructs sharp images from the event data and then employs a cross-domain pose estimation module based on the Visual Geometry Transformer (VGGT) to obtain robust initialization for 3DGS. During optimization, we employ a structure-driven event loss and view-specific consistency regularizers to mitigate the ill-posed behavior of traditional event losses and deblurring losses, ensuring both stable and high-fidelity reconstruction. We further contribute AsyncEv-Deblur, a new high-resolution RGB-Event dataset captured with our asynchronous system. Experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both our challenging dataset and existing benchmarks, substantially improving reconstruction robustness under severe motion blur. Project page: https://openimaginglab.github.io/AsyncEvGS/

</details>


### [40] [Closed-Form Linear-Probe Dataset Distillation for Pre-trained Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07194)
*Bincheng Peng,Guang Li,Ping Liu,Takahiro Ogawa,Miki Haseyama*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Closed-Form Linear-Probe Dataset Distillation (CLP-DD), a method that improves dataset distillation for frozen pre-trained encoders in visual transfer learning, demonstrating significant efficiency and performance gains compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance dataset distillation by leveraging the unique properties of frozen-feature linear probing, which have been overlooked by standard distillation methods that typically focus on from-scratch training.

Method: CLP-DD employs a bilevel formulation to compute the induced linear probe directly from the synthetic dataset using a sample-space kernel ridge solver, updating synthetic images via a temperature-scaled softmax cross-entropy.

Result: CLP-DD shows substantial improvements in performance over existing methods like LGM on ImageNet-100 and ImageNet-1K, achieving notable speed and memory efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed method not only significantly enhances dataset distillation effectiveness but also reduces computational resources, marking a key advancement in the field.

Abstract: Dataset distillation compresses a large training set into a small synthetic set that preserves downstream training utility. While most existing methods target training networks from scratch, modern visual transfer learning often uses frozen pre-trained encoders followed by lightweight linear probing. Existing distillation methods for this setting either unroll iterative linear-probe updates with trajectory-based gradient matching, or rely on closed-form formulations originally designed for from-scratch training with neural-tangent-kernel (NTK) approximations. Neither route exploits the fact that frozen-feature linear probing admits a closed-form solution determined directly by the pre-trained features themselves, with no infinite-width approximation and no inner-loop trajectory. We propose Closed-Form Linear-Probe Dataset Distillation (CLP-DD), a bilevel formulation that computes the linear probe induced by the synthetic set with a sample-space kernel ridge solver. The synthetic images are then updated by evaluating this induced classifier on real features through a temperature-scaled softmax cross-entropy, where the classifier columns act as learned class anchors in feature space. We further show that the choice of outer objective is decisive: pairing the closed-form inner solver with a standard MSE outer loss substantially underperforms trajectory-based methods, while the discriminative outer loss closes most of the gap. On ImageNet-100 with four pre-trained backbones, CLP-DD substantially improves over LGM without DSA and approaches LGM with DSA at a fraction of the computational cost. On ImageNet-1K, CLP-DD matches or surpasses LGM with DSA on three of four backbones while running roughly $14\times$ faster and using less than one-eighth of the GPU memory.

</details>


### [41] [See Tomorrow, Act Today: Foresight-Driven Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07195)
*Bozhou Zhang,Nan Song,Yuang Wang,Jiankang Deng,Xiatian Zhu,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ForeSight is a new planning framework for autonomous driving that emphasizes future scene imagination over reactive decision-making, leading to improved navigation in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance autonomous driving decision-making by transitioning from reactive strategies based on current observations to anticipatory techniques that simulate future scenarios.

Method: ForeSight employs a two-stage process: first, it generates plausible future visual worlds with a pretrained world model; second, it plans actions based on these imagined futures, emphasizing anticipation in decision-making.

Result: ForeSight has been rigorously tested against existing state-of-the-art methods on NAVSIM and nuScenes, demonstrating significant performance improvements thanks to its foresight-driven planning approach.

Conclusion: The proposed framework allows for more effective navigation in complex, interactive environments by grounding decisions in anticipated future contexts.

Abstract: Current end-to-end autonomous driving planners are fundamentally reactive: they condition on historical and present observations to predict future actions. We argue that autonomous agents should instead imagine future scenes before deciding, just as human drivers mentally simulate ``what will happen next" before acting. We introduce ForeSight, a foundation world model centric planning framework that reframes autonomous driving as anticipatory decision-making. Rather than treating world models as auxiliary components, ForeSight makes future scene imagination the primary driver of action prediction. Our approach operates in two stages: (1) generating plausible future visual worlds via a pretrained world model, and (2) planning actions conditioned on these imagined futures. This paradigm shift from ``what should I do now?" to ``what will happen, and how should I respond?" enables genuinely anticipatory rather than reactive planning. By grounding decisions in anticipated contexts rather than present observations alone, ForeSight navigates dynamic, interactive scenarios more effectively. Extensive experiments on NAVSIM and nuScenes demonstrate that explicit future imagination significantly outperforms previous state-of-the-art alternatives, validating our foresight-driven approach.

</details>


### [42] [Operating Within the Operational Design Domain: Zero-Shot Perception with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07649)
*Berkehan Ünal,Dierend Hauke,Fazlija Dren,Plachetka Christopher*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper investigates the use of vision-language models (VLMs) for classifying and detecting elements of the Operational Design Domain (ODD) in autonomous systems, highlighting their potential as adaptable zero-shot sensors and providing strategies for optimal performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable the practical adoption of autonomous systems, ensuring adherence to safety regulations reliant on accurate perception of the Operational Design Domain (ODD) is vital.

Method: Conducted empirical studies on zero-shot ODD classification and detection with four VLMs, analyzed failures, evaluated optimization strategies, and created reusable prompting templates.

Result: Definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition yielded the best performance, while alternative methods diminished recall.

Conclusion: This research supports the implementation of VLMs for ODD perception, enhancing transparency and safety in autonomous driving applications.

Abstract: Over the last few years, research on autonomous systems has matured to such a degree that the field is increasingly well-positioned to translate research into practical, stakeholder-driven use cases across well-defined domains. However, for a wide-scale practical adoption of autonomous systems, adherence to safety regulations is crucial. Many regulations are influenced by the Operational Design Domain (ODD), which defines the specific conditions in which an autonomous agent can function. This is especially relevant for Automated Driving Systems (ADS), as a dependable perception of ODD elements is essential for safe implementation and auditing. Vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual recognition and language reasoning, functioning without task-specific training data, which makes them suitable for adaptable ODD perception. To assess whether VLMs can function as zero-shot "ODD sensors" that adapt to evolving definitions, we contribute (i) an empirical study of zero-shot ODD classification and detection using four VLMs on a custom dataset and Mapillary Vistas, along with failure analyses; (ii) an ablation of zero-shot optimization strategies with a cost-performance overview; and (iii) a suite of reusable prompting templates with guidance for adaptation. Our findings indicate that definition-anchored chain-of-thought prompting with persona decomposition performs best, while other methods may result in reduced recall. Overall, our results pave the way for transparent and effective ODD-based perception in safety-critical applications.

</details>


### [43] [From Pixels to Primitives: Scene Change Detection in 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07203)
*Chamuditha Jayanga Galappaththige,Jason Lai,Timothy Patten,Donald Dansereau,Niko Suenderhauf,Dimity Miller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GD-DIFF improves scene change detection by utilizing primitive attributes for identifying changes, resulting in multi-view consistency and separate scoring for structural and surface-level changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance scene change detection by addressing the limitations of existing render-then-compare methods that rely heavily on pixel-based comparisons.

Method: The authors introduce GD-DIFF, which utilizes primitive attributes (position, anisotropic covariance, color) and incorporates a model for geometric and photometric drift, alongside a per-primitive observability term.

Result: GD-DIFF achieves a 17% improvement in mean Intersection over Union when compared to the previous state-of-the-art method.

Conclusion: The method demonstrates effective scene change detection by operating directly on primitives, allowing for clear identification of the type of changes without needing additional supervision.

Abstract: Scene change detection methods built on Gaussian splatting universally follow a render-then-compare paradigm: the pre-change scene is rendered into 2D and compared against post-change images via pixel or feature residuals. This change detection problem with Gaussian Splatting has been treated as a question about pixels; we treat it as a question about primitives. We provide direct evidence that native primitive attributes alone -- position, anisotropic covariance, and color -- carry sufficient signal for scene change detection. What makes primitive-space comparison hard is the under-constrained nature of Gaussian splatting representation: independent optimizations yield primitive solutions whose count, positions, shapes, and colors differ even where nothing has changed. We address this challenge with anisotropic models of geometric and photometric drift, complemented by a per-primitive observability term that reflects the extent to which each Gaussian is constrained by the camera geometry. Operating directly on primitives gives our method, GD-DIFF, two properties that distinguish it from render-then-compare methods. First, change maps are multi-view consistent by construction, where prior work had to learn this through an additional optimization objective. Second, geometric and appearance changes are scored separately, identifying not just where but what kind of change occurred, distinguishing structural changes (e.g., an added object) from surface-level ones (e.g., a color change) without supervision or external model dependencies. On real-world benchmarks, GS-DIFF surpasses the prior state-of-the-art approach by approximatelt 17% in mean Intersection over Union.

</details>


### [44] [LoHGNet: Infrared Small Target Detection through Lorentz Geometric Encoding with High-Order Relation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07213)
*Qianwen Ma,Yang Xu,Shangwei Deng,Xiaobo Li,Haofeng Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LoHGNet is a novel IRSTD network that utilizes Lorentz geometric encoding and high-order relation learning to improve detection of small infrared targets amidst cluttered backgrounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional IRSTD methods struggle due to limited feature representation and background clutter, necessitating a more effective approach.

Method: LoHGNet employs Lorentz manifold based feature learning and integrates a Geometric Attention Guided Lorentz Residual Convolution Module for enhanced target modeling, followed by a High-Order Relation Learning Module that utilizes hypergraph construction to improve contextual relationships between targets and backgrounds.

Result: Experiments on three datasets indicate that LoHGNet consistently outperforms existing IRSTD methods in detection accuracy and adaptability to complex environments.

Conclusion: LoHGNet presents a promising advancement in IRSTD techniques by incorporating Lorentz geometric principles and relational learning to facilitate better target detection.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) remains challenging due to the scarcity of useful target cues and the presence of severe background clutter. Most current methods rely on conventional feature learning and local interaction modeling, where features are represented in Euclidean space. However, such designs may still be limited in describing the subtle differences of weak targets and the contextual relations between targets and backgrounds. To address these limitations, we propose LoHGNet, an IRSTD network that integrates Lorentz geometric encoding with high-order relation learning. By introducing Lorentz manifold based feature learning, LoHGNet offers a different feature representation from conventional IRSTD methods and provides new discriminative cues for IRSTD. Specifically, a Lorentz encoding branch is constructed with the Geometric Attention Guided Lorentz Residual Convolution Module (GA-LRCM) to perform feature modeling under hyperbolic geometric constraints and enhance the hierarchical geometric representation capability of weak targets. Subsequently, the hyperbolic features are mapped into the Euclidean tangent space through logarithmic mapping, and a High-Order Relation Learning Module (HORL) is designed to model the high-order contextual dependencies between targets and backgrounds via hypergraph construction, thereby improving target discrimination in complex backgrounds. Experimental results on three datasets demonstrate that the proposed LoHGNet achieves competitive performance in both detection accuracy and adaptability to complex scenes. The code will be available at https://github.com/Kingwin97.

</details>


### [45] [Text-to-CAD Evaluation with CADTests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07807)
*Dimitrios Mallis,Marco Wang,Ahmet Serdar Karadeniz,Elisa Ricci,Anis Kacem,Djamila Aouada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces CADTestBench, a new benchmark for evaluating Text-to-CAD systems through automated testing with CADTests, which assess the compliance of generated models with input specifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited work on evaluating Text-to-CAD systems and improve assessment of CAD model generation performance.

Method: Development of CADTestBench, a benchmark using executable CADTests for verification of CAD models against input prompts.

Result: Comprehensive benchmarking of existing Text-to-CAD methods, showing that CADTests can improve model generation and achieve better baseline performance than current methods.

Conclusion: CADTestBench enhances evaluation of Text-to-CAD systems and provides a new way to guide the generation of CAD models, with resources available publicly.

Abstract: Text-to-CAD has recently emerged as an important task with the potential to substantially accelerate design workflows. Despite its significance, there has been surprisingly little work on Text-to-CAD evaluation, and assessing CAD model generation performance remains a considerable challenge. In this work, we introduce a new evaluation perspective for Text-to-CAD based on automated testing. We propose CADTestBench, the first test-based benchmark for Text-to-CAD, based on CADTests, executable software tests that verify whether a generated CAD model satisfies the geometric and topological requirements of the input prompt. Using CADTestBench, we conduct comprehensive benchmarking of recent Text-to-CAD methods and further demonstrate that CADTests can also guide CAD model generation, yielding simple baselines that surpass performance of current methods. CADTestBench code and data are available at GitHub and Hugging Face dataset.

</details>


### [46] [6D Pose Estimation via Keypoint Heatmap Regression with RGB-D Residual Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08059)
*Ismail Aljosevic,Amir Masoud Almasi,Ana Parovic,Ashkan Shafiei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a modular framework for 6D pose estimation using keypoint heatmap regression, combining YOLOv10m and a ResNet18-based network and enhancing it with depth data to improve accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in accurate 6D pose estimation by leveraging keypoints from heatmaps and incorporating depth information.

Method: The method integrates YOLOv10m for object detection and a ResNet18-based network to produce 2D heatmaps from RGB images, followed by keypoint extraction for pose estimation using the PnP RANSAC algorithm.

Result: The RGB-only model achieved a mean ADD-based accuracy of 84.50%, while the RGB-D fusion model improved this to 92.41% on the LINEMOD dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of depth data.

Conclusion: The proposed framework shows significant improvements in pose estimation accuracy through keypoint selection and the incorporation of depth information.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a modular framework for 6D pose estimation based on keypoint heatmap regression. Our approach combines YOLOv10m for object detection with a ResNet18-based network that predicts 2D heatmaps from RGB images. Keypoints extracted from these heatmaps are used to estimate the 6D object pose via the PnP RANSAC algorithm. We compare different keypoint selection strategies to assess their impact on pose accuracy. Additionally, we extend the baseline by incorporating depth data using a cross-fusion architecture, which enables interaction between RGB and depth features at multiple stages. We further explore general training improvements, such as experimenting with activation functions and learning rate scheduling strategies to improve model performance. Our best RGB-only model achieved a mean ADD-based accuracy of 84.50%, while the RGB-D fusion model reached 92.41% on the LINEMOD dataset. The code is available at https://github.com/ameermasood/HeatNet.

</details>


### [47] [DINO-MVR: Multi-View Readout of Frozen DINOv3 for Annotation-Efficient Medical Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07221)
*Wei Jiang,Feng Liu,Nan Ye,Hongfu Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DINO-MVR is a novel framework for medical segmentation that leverages frozen DINOv3 features and lightweight probes to enhance performance with minimal annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve medical segmentation by utilizing frozen DINOv3 features without extensive fine-tuning or complex decoders, especially in scenarios with limited annotations.

Method: DINO-MVR uses lightweight MLP probes trained on specific transformer block features of the frozen DINOv3, employing multi-view readout with test-time augmentations and entropy-weighted fusion for improved segmentation accuracy.

Result: DINO-MVR achieves high Dice scores: 0.895 on Kvasir-SEG, 0.897 on ISIC 2018, and 0.908 on BraTS FLAIR, demonstrating effective segmentation with minimal annotations.

Conclusion: The approach indicates that leveraging frozen self-supervised vision backbones in conjunction with a multi-view readout can yield accurate medical segmentation results even when annotation is scarce.

Abstract: Adapting foundation models to medical segmentation typically requires either backbone fine-tuning or high-capacity task-specific decoders, both of which are difficult to fit reliably when annotations are scarce. We show that frozen DINOv3 features already contain useful structural and boundary cues for medical segmentation, and that the main bottleneck lies in how these features are read out. We propose DINO-MVR, a Multi-View Readout framework for annotation-efficient medical segmentation. DINO-MVR trains only lightweight MLP probes on features from the final three transformer blocks of a frozen DINOv3 backbone, without updating the backbone itself. At inference, each input is interpreted through complementary resolutions and test-time augmentations, whose probability maps are combined by entropy-weighted fusion and refined with simple spatial regularization. For volumetric inputs, Gaussian z-axis smoothing further improves inter-slice consistency. Under fixed evaluation protocols on endoscopy, dermoscopy, and MRI benchmarks, DINO-MVR achieves strong readout-only performance, including 0.895 Dice on Kvasir-SEG, 0.897 Dice on ISIC 2018, and 0.908 Dice on BraTS FLAIR whole-tumor segmentation. With only five annotated BraTS patients, it recovers 98.4% of the performance obtained by the 40-patient BraTS reference run. These results suggest that frozen self-supervised vision backbones can support accurate medical segmentation when paired with an effective multi-view readout.

</details>


### [48] [CASCADE: Context-Aware Relaxation for Speculative Image Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07230)
*Selin Yildirim,Subhajit Dutta Chowdhury,Mohammad Mahdi Kamani,Vikram Appia,Deming Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents CASCADE, a method that enhances the efficiency of autoregressive image synthesis through improved speculative decoding, achieving significant speedups without compromising quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the slow and computationally demanding nature of autoregressive image generation, particularly in relation to existing speculative decoding methods that are less efficient than those for text generation.

Method: The paper formalizes two properties—semantic interchangeability and convergence—based on redundancies found in the target model's hidden states, enabling acceptance relaxation during generation. It incorporates these redundancy signals into drafter performance enhancements with minor adjustments.

Result: CASCADE demonstrates up to 3.6x acceleration in drafter-based speculative decoding across various text-to-image models, without sacrificing image quality or text-prompt fidelity.

Conclusion: The proposed method significantly improves the speed and efficiency of image generation models, setting a new standard in speculative decoding performance.

Abstract: Autoregressive generation is a powerful approach for high-fidelity image synthesis, but it remains computationally demanding and slow even on the most advanced accelerators. While speculative decoding has been explored to mitigate this bottleneck, existing approaches fail to achieve efficiency gains comparable to those observed in text generation. A key limitation is the target model's high uncertainty during image generation, which leads to high draft token rejection rates. In this work, we identify previously overlooked patterns in the target model's behavior that emerge naturally in tree-based speculative decoding. Specifically, we formalize two properties, semantic interchangeability and convergence, arising from the redundancies in the target model's hidden state representations. By capturing these redundancies across the depth and breadth of the predicted token tree, our method identifies principled opportunities for acceptance relaxation without requiring additional training. Additionally, we enhance standalone drafter performance by injecting the redundancy signals from the target model into drafter training with minimal modification. We evaluate our approach across multiple text-to-image models and drafter architectures. Results show that CASCADE achieves state-of-the-art speedups for drafter-based speculative decoding, with up to 3.6x acceleration, while maintaining image quality and text-prompt fidelity.

</details>


### [49] [Towards multi-modal forgery representation learning for AI-generated video detection and localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07232)
*Dat Le,Khoa Nguyen,Xin Wang,Shu Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel multi-modal detection framework for AI-generated video forgeries, integrating semantic, visual, and audio analysis to enhance detection accuracy and temporal localization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing prevalence of AI-generated videos presents risks of misuse and semantic distortion, necessitating reliable detection tools.

Method: The paper introduces a new architecture combining a semantic branch, a spatio-temporal visual branch, and a multi-scale audio branch to analyze manipulated video content.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates superior performance compared to state-of-the-art detection techniques, effectively addressing limitations in existing approaches.

Conclusion: The integration of multiple modalities facilitates improved detection and localization of AI-generated video forgeries, highlighting the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative AI have democratized video creation at scale. AI-generated videos, including partially manipulated clips across visual and audio channels, pose escalating risks of semantic distortion and misuse, which motivates the need for reliable detection tools. Most existing AI-generated video detectors remain limited by single- or partial-modality of data modeling and the lack of fine-grained temporal forgery localization. To address these challenges, our primary novelty introduces a core architecture that jointly integrates an LMM semantic branch with a spatio-temporal (ST) visual branch and a multi-scale partial-spoof (PS) audio branch. This multi-modal approach enables simultaneous detection and fine-grained temporal localization of partially manipulated AI-generated video forgeries. Extensive experiments show that this approach outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [50] [Hard to Read, Easy to Jailbreak: How Visual Degradation Bypasses MLLM Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07250)
*Zhixue Song,Boyan Han,Yiwei Wang,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper examines the vulnerabilities in using visual context compression for MLLMs, identifying that reduced image resolution can lead to security issues. It proposes a strategy to improve safety while processing visual inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to highlight a critical vulnerability in the use of visual context compression for MLLMs, particularly how lowering resolution impacts model safety.

Method: The authors conducted experiments to analyze how varying resolutions affect model safety and propose a strategy called 'Structured Cognitive Offloading' to mitigate identified risks.

Result: The experiments demonstrate a significant decline in safety defenses of state-of-the-art models as image resolution decreases, even when the text remains legible.

Conclusion: The findings reveal important risks associated with vision-based compression techniques, stressing the need for secure designs in future MLLMs.

Abstract: Recent advancements in visual context compression enable MLLMs to process ultra-long contexts efficiently by rendering text into images. However, we identify a critical vulnerability inherent to this paradigm: lowering image resolution inadvertently catalyzes jailbreaking. Our experiments reveal that the safety defenses of SOTA models deteriorate sharply as resolution degrades, surprisingly persisting even when text remains legible. We attribute this to ``Cognitive Overload'', hypothesizing that the effort required to decipher degraded inputs diverts attentional resources from safety auditing. This phenomenon is consistent across various visual perturbations, including noise and geometric distortion. To address this, we propose a simple ``Structured Cognitive Offloading'' strategy that mitigates these risks by enforcing a serialized pipeline to decouple visual transcription from safety assessment. Our work exposes a significant risk in vision-based compression and provides critical insights for the secure design of future MLLMs.

</details>


### [51] [LENS: Low-Frequency Eigen Noise Shaping for Efficient Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07253)
*Haewon Jeon,Si-Hyeon Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LENS introduces an efficient noise modulation framework using low-frequency components to enhance image generation in diffusion models while significantly reducing computational costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind LENS is to improve the image quality of distilled diffusion models that face trade-offs between accelerated generation and quality degradation, particularly through effective noise modulation.

Method: LENS employs a low-dimensional subspace for noise modulation, focusing on low-frequency components that significantly impact image structure and fidelity. A lightweight network is utilized for selective modulation of these components.

Result: LENS achieves enhanced image quality metrics while drastically reducing computational requirements—specifically, a 400-700$	imes$ reduction in FLOPs, a 25-75$	imes$ reduction in model parameters, and a 10-20$	imes$ decrease in inference-time overhead compared to existing techniques.

Conclusion: The proposed LENS framework successfully balances image quality and computational efficiency in diffusion models, making it advantageous for practical applications.

Abstract: Distilled diffusion models accelerate image generation by reducing the number of denoising steps, but often suffer from degraded image quality. To mitigate this trade-off, test-time optimization methods improve quality, yet their iterative nature incurs substantial computational overhead and leads to slow inference, limiting practical usability. Recent hypernetwork-based approaches amortize this process during training, but still require costly noise modulation in high-dimensional latent spaces. In this work, we propose LENS (Low-frequency Eigen Noise Shaping), an efficient noise modulation framework that operates in a low-dimensional subspace. Our approach is motivated by the observation that low-frequency components of the noise largely determine the global structure and visual fidelity of generated images. Based on this observation, we provide a theoretical justification for restricting modulation to the low-frequency subspace and derive a principled training objective. Building on this, LENS employs a lightweight, standalone network to selectively modulate these components, enabling efficient and targeted noise modulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LENS achieves competitive image quality while reducing FLOPs by 400-700$\times$, model parameters by 25-75$\times$, and inference-time overhead by 10-20$\times$ compared to prior methods.

</details>


### [52] [High-Fidelity Surface Splatting-Based 3D Reconstruction from Multi-View Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07254)
*Nandhana Sunil,Abhirami R Iyer,Avirup Mandal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents an enhanced multi-view mesh reconstruction approach that improves high-frequency geometry recovery using a novel polynomial kernel and stochastic regularization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in high-frequency geometry recovery from sparse observations in multi-view mesh reconstruction.

Method: The paper introduces a compact polynomial kernel with local support, enhancing existing Implicit Moving Least Squares (IMLS) methods, paired with stochastic regularization and Laplacian filtering.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in surface reconstruction and rendering, yielding more accurate geometry and sharper visuals from multi-view data, particularly in high-frequency detail.

Conclusion: The new kernel and regularization techniques significantly improve the preservation of high-frequency structures and stabilize the optimization process.

Abstract: Multi-view mesh reconstruction remains a core challenge in computer graphics and vision, especially for recovering high-frequency geometry from sparse observations. Recent methods such as 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) and Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) rely on post-processing for mesh extraction, thereby limiting joint optimization of geometry and appearance. Implicit Moving Least Squares (IMLS) instead enables direct conversion of point clouds into signed distance and texture fields, supporting end-to-end reconstruction and rendering. However, existing IMLS formulations use exponential kernels that struggle with high-frequency detail. We introduce a compact polynomial kernel with local support and greater flexibility, allowing better control over frequency content and improved geometric fidelity. To further enhance fine details, we incorporate stochastic regularization with Laplacian filtering. Together, these improve the preservation of high-frequency structure while maintaining stable optimization. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance in both surface reconstruction and rendering, yielding more accurate geometry and sharper visuals from multi-view data.

</details>


### [53] [TAS-LoRA: Transformer Architecture Search with Mixture-of-LoRA Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07256)
*Jeimin Jeon,Hyunju Lee,Bumsub Ham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TAS-LoRA is an advanced method for automated vision transformer architecture search that addresses feature collapse by using low-rank adaptation techniques, improving performance in various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Transformer Architecture Search methods face feature collapse, limiting subnet learning due to shared weights, necessitating an improved approach for optimal feature extraction.

Method: TAS-LoRA employs low-rank adaptation and a Mixture-of-LoRAExperts strategy with dynamic routing to enhance subnet-specific feature learning and incorporates diverse initialization techniques for better training outcomes.

Result: Experimental results on ImageNet and multiple transfer learning benchmarks demonstrate that TAS-LoRA effectively reduces the feature collapse problem, leading to significant performance improvements over existing methods.

Conclusion: TAS-LoRA presents a novel solution to the limitations of current TAS methodologies, showing enhanced capability in feature learning and overall architecture performance.

Abstract: Transformer architecture search (TAS) discovers optimal vision transformer (ViT) architectures automatically, reducing human effort to manually design ViTs. However, existing TAS methods suffer from the feature collapse problem, where subnets within a supernet fail to learn subnet-specific features, mainly due to the shared weights in a supernet, limiting the performance of individual subnets. To address this, we propose TAS-LoRA, a novel method that introduces parameter-efficient low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to enable subnet-specific feature learning, while maintaining computational efficiency. TAS-LoRA incorporates a Mixture-of-LoRAExperts (MoLE) strategy, where a lightweight router dynamically assigns LoRA experts based on subnet architectures, and introduces a group-wise router initialization technique to encourage diverse feature learning across experts early in training. Extensive experiments on ImageNet and several transfer learning benchmarks, including CIFAR-10/100, Flowers, CARS, and INAT-19, demonstrate that TAS-LoRA mitigates feature collapse effectively, improving performance over state-of-the-art TAS methods significantly.

</details>


### [54] [Adaptive Subspace Projection for Generative Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07257)
*Van-Anh Nguyen,Anh Tuan Bui,Tamas Abraham,Junae Kim,Amardeep Kaur,Rollin Omari,Thuy-Trang Vu,Dinh Phung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper addresses the semantic collapsing problem in generative personalization by introducing a method called AdaptSP, which mitigates semantic drift during personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to solve the semantic collapsing problem (SCP) in generative personalization, where important context is overlooked due to dominant learned concepts.

Method: The proposed method, AdaptSP, utilizes stable pre-trained embeddings and isolates semantic drift by projecting it onto a specific low-dimensional subspace without retraining.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that AdaptSP significantly enhances prompt fidelity and contextual alignment.

Conclusion: The findings confirm that precise adjustment of embeddings can alleviate SCP while preserving the identity of the subject.

Abstract: Generative personalization often suffers from the semantic collapsing problem (SCP), where a learned personalized concept overpowers the rest of the text prompt, causing the model to ignore important contextual details. To address this, we first analyze the underlying cause, revealing that the semantic drift responsible for SCP is not random but is concentrated within a specific low-dimensional subspace. We also discover that the personalization process perturbs the embedding of the original base concept, making it an unstable reference point. Based on these insights, we introduce Test-time Embedding Adjustment with Adaptive Subspace Projection (AdaptSP), a training-free method that uses the stable, pre-trained embedding as an anchor. AdaptSP isolates the semantic drift and projects it onto the identified subspace, performing a precise adjustment that mitigates SCP while maintaining the subject identity. Our experiments show that this targeted approach significantly improves prompt fidelity and contextual alignment.

</details>


### [55] [From Clouds to Hallucinations: Atmospheric Retrieval Hijacking in Remote Sensing Vision-Language RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07273)
*Jiaju Han,Chao Li,Chengyin Hu,Qike Zhang,Xuemeng Sun,Xin Wang,Fengyu Zhang,Xiang Chen,Yiwei Wei,Jiahuan Long,Jiujiang Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CloudWeb is a novel attack on the retrieval stage of multimodal RAG systems, enhancing remote sensing image queries by using atmospheric modifications to input images. It effectively shifts the retrieval outcomes by optimizing adversarial patterns, resulting in significant improvements in task performance and indicating a previously unnoticed vulnerability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore input-space threats in retrieval-stage of remote sensing multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, which have been underexplored compared to other modes of attack.

Method: CloudWeb modifies input images by overlaying optimized cloud- and haze-like patterns to influence the retrieval process, while maintaining the integrity of the retriever and knowledge base. The optimization aligns adversarial embeddings with target atmospheric evidence and regulates output quality.

Result: CloudWeb significantly outperforms baseline retrieval methods and random perturbations, with a notable increase in retrieval accuracy for weather-related evidence, demonstrating the effectiveness of the attack strategy.

Conclusion: The study reveals that benign-looking atmospheric alterations can compromise the retrieval process in multimodal RAG systems, highlighting a critical vulnerability in evidence retrieval that can affect task performance.

Abstract: Multimodal RAG systems increasingly rely on vision-language retrievers to ground visual queries in external textual evidence. Existing adversarial studies on RAG mainly manipulate the retrieval corpus or memory, while attacks on vision-language and remote sensing models typically target end-task predictions. Input-space threats to the evidence retrieval stage of remote sensing multimodal RAG remain underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce CloudWeb, an atmospheric retrieval hijacking attack that modifies only the input image while keeping the retriever, generator, and knowledge base fixed at deployment. CloudWeb overlays parameterized cloud- and haze-like patterns on remote sensing images and optimizes them with a retrieval-oriented objective that pulls adversarial image embeddings toward target atmospheric evidence, suppresses source-scene evidence, enforces rank separation, and regularizes naturalness and coverage. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of retrieval-stage atmospheric evidence hijacking in remote sensing multimodal RAG. We evaluate CloudWeb on a seven-dataset remote sensing RAG benchmark with five CLIP-style retrievers, including GeoRSCLIP, RemoteCLIP, OpenAI CLIP, and OpenCLIP, together with downstream vision-language generators. Across retrievers, CloudWeb consistently outperforms clean retrieval, handcrafted atmospheric baselines, random cloud perturbations, and fixed variants in injecting weather-related evidence into top-ranked results. On GeoRSCLIP ViT-B/32, Weather@5 increases from 0.71\% to 43.29\%. Downstream generation further shows measurable weather hallucination and semantic shift, indicating that retrieval-stage hijacking can propagate to the final RAG response. These findings reveal a practical failure mode: natural-looking atmospheric changes can compromise evidence retrieval before generation begins.

</details>


### [56] [SplatWeaver: Learning to Allocate Gaussian Primitives for Generalizable Novel View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07287)
*Yecong Wan,Fan Li,Mingwen Shao,Wangmeng Zuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SplatWeaver enhances novel view synthesis by adaptively allocating Gaussian primitives based on scene complexity, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve Gaussian allocation in novel view synthesis, addressing the inefficiencies of fixed primitive assignment in varying scene complexities.

Method: SplatWeaver employs cardinality Gaussian experts and a pixel-level routing scheme to dynamically allocate Gaussian primitives, favoring complex scene regions.

Result: SplatWeaver demonstrates superior performance in rendering quality and efficiency, with a more adaptive allocation of Gaussian primitives tailored to scene complexity.

Conclusion: The innovative routing process and guidance mechanisms in SplatWeaver contribute to more accurate and efficient novel view renderings, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art techniques.

Abstract: Generalizable novel view synthesis aims to render unseen views from uncalibrated input images without requiring per-scene optimization. Recent feed-forward approaches based on 3D Gaussian Splatting have achieved promising efficiency and rendering quality. However, most of them assign a fixed number of Gaussians to each pixel or voxel, ignoring the spatially varying complexity of real-world scenes. Such uniform allocation often wastes Gaussian primitives in smooth regions while providing insufficient capacity for fine structures, complex geometry, and high-frequency details. This motivates us to predict region-dependent primitive cardinalities rather than impose a fixed primitive budget everywhere, enabling a more expressive yet compact 3D scene representation. Therefore, we propose SplatWeaver, a generalizable novel view synthesis framework that is able to dynamically allocate Gaussian primitives over different regions in a feed-forward manner. Specifically, SplatWeaver introduces cardinality Gaussian experts and a pixel-level routing scheme, wherein each expert specializes in producing a specific number of primitives from 0 to M, and the routing scheme coordinates these experts to adaptively determine how many Gaussian primitives should be allocated to each spatial location. Moreover, SplatWeaver incorporates a high-frequency prior with attendant guidance module and routing regularization to stabilize expert selection and promote complexity-aware allocation. By leveraging high-frequency structural cues, the routing process is encouraged to assign more Gaussian primitives to fine structures, complex geometry, and textured regions, while suppressing redundant primitives in smooth areas. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios show that SplatWeaver consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, delivering more faithful novel-view renderings with fewer Gaussian primitives.

</details>


### [57] [Sword: Style-Robust World Models as Simulators via Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping for VLA Policy Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07288)
*Jiaxuan Gao,Yongjian Guo,Zhong Guan,Wen Huang,Wanlun Ma,Xi Xiao,Junwu Xiong,Sheng Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents Sword, a robust World Model framework that improves the stability and performance of Vision-Language-Action models by addressing issues of poor generalization and long-horizon error accumulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the reliability and performance of World Models as simulators for Vision-Language-Action tasks, addressing challenges in generalization and error propagation.

Method: Sword introduces Structure-Guided Style Augmentation for disentangling visual textures from task dynamics and Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping for maintaining training-inference consistency with low memory usage.

Result: Sword significantly outperforms the baseline WoVR on the LIBERO benchmark across multiple metrics, including generalization, generation quality, and robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed enhancements in Sword lead to improved reliability and performance of World Models in simulating interactive environments for Vision-Language-Action tasks.

Abstract: The integration of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models with World Models has gained increasing attention. One representative approach treats learned World Models as generative simulators, enabling policy optimization entirely within "imagination." However, when deployed as simulators for specific environments such as the LIBERO benchmark, existing World Models often suffer from poor generalization and long-horizon error accumulation. During closed-loop rollouts, these models are highly sensitive to initial-state perturbations; minor changes in color, illumination, and other visual factors can trigger cascading hallucinations, leading to severe blurriness or overexposure. Moreover, long-horizon error accumulation further degrades the quality and fidelity of predicted future states. These issues limit the reliability of World Models as simulators. To mitigate these problems, we propose Sword, a robust World Model framework. Our method introduces Structure-Guided Style Augmentation to disentangle the visual textures of interactive environments from task-relevant dynamics, thereby improving generalization. We further propose Dynamic Latent Bootstrapping, which maintains consistency between training and inference while keeping memory consumption low. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that our method significantly outperforms the baseline WoVR in terms of generalization, generation quality, robustness, fidelity, and the success rate of reinforcement-learning post-training for VLA models.

</details>


### [58] [EgoPro-Bench: Benchmarking Personalized Proactive Interaction in Egocentric Video Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.07299)
*Dongchuan Ran,Linyu Ou,Xueheng Li,Wenwen Tong,Chenxu Guo,Hewei Guo,Kaibing Wang,Lewei Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EgoPro-Bench is a new benchmark designed to enhance proactive interaction capabilities of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using streaming egocentric videos, incorporating diverse user profiles for better human-machine interaction (HMI).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing MLLMs in proactive user assistance and the evaluation of personalized, timely interactions in dynamic environments.

Method: EgoPro-Bench utilizes 2,400 evaluation videos and over 12,000 training videos with simulated user profiles to create diverse user intentions and improve high-fidelity HMI. It includes a specialized evaluation protocol and metrics, focusing on efficient reasoning in low-latency video interactions.

Result: The evaluation shows that EgoPro-Bench significantly improves MLLMs' intention understanding and enhances timing accuracy for effective HMI.

Conclusion: EgoPro-Bench provides a foundational framework for developing next-generation user-centric proactive interactive agents.

Abstract: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remain primarily reactive, failing to continuously perceive environments or proactively assist users.
